_meta.source: Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (eCFR), Title 15, Subtitle B, Chapter VII, Subchapter C; Federal Register
_meta.citation: 15 CFR Parts 730–774 (Export Administration Regulations); eCFR at https://www.ecfr.gov/
_meta.retrieved_by: Manual seed — run scripts/refresh_rules.py to update
_meta.notes: These are paraphrased summaries of CFR text, not verbatim reproductions. Summaries are provided for orientation purposes only. Always consult the current official eCFR text at https://www.ecfr.gov/ for authoritative regulatory language. The EAR is amended frequently; this snapshot may be outdated.

sections.title: General Information — Scope of the EAR
sections.summary: Part 730 explains the purpose and scope of the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The EAR implements the Export Control Reform Act of 2018 (ECRA) and controls exports, reexports, and transfers (in-country) of commodities, software, and technology. The EAR applies to items subject to US jurisdiction based on US origin, US content, or manufacture using US equipment or software. The BIS administers the EAR on behalf of the Commerce Department.
sections.notes: Paraphrased summary — not verbatim. Consult eCFR for authoritative text.

sections.title: Scope of the EAR — Subject to EAR; De Minimis; FDPR; Deemed Export
sections.summary: Part 734 defines what is 'subject to the EAR'. US-origin items are always subject. Foreign items with US-controlled content exceeding de minimis thresholds (typically 10% by value for most countries, 25% for some; 0% for E:1 destinations) may be subject. The Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR) at § 734.9 extends jurisdiction to foreign-made items that are direct products of controlled US technology or software, or produced on equipment that is itself the direct product of controlled US-origin technology. Deemed exports — releases of controlled technology or source code to foreign nationals — are treated as exports to the national's home country under § 734.13.
sections.notes: De minimis thresholds are 0% for E:1 countries. FDPR was substantially expanded for China advanced computing controls (Oct. 2022) and Russia (Feb. 2022). Paraphrased — consult eCFR.

sections.title: Deemed Export — Release of Technology to Foreign Nationals
sections.summary: A 'deemed export' occurs when controlled technology or source code is released to a foreign national in the United States or abroad. 'Release' includes visual inspection, oral exchanges, and access to software or data. The release is deemed an export to the foreign national's most recent country of citizenship or permanent residence. Cloud-based access to EAR-controlled software by a foreign national constitutes a deemed export.
sections.notes: Critical for SaaS/IaaS platforms and cloud AI services. KYC procedures must account for deemed-export risk. Paraphrased — consult eCFR.

sections.title: Restrictions on Certain Military End-Uses and End-Users
sections.summary: Section 744.21 prohibits the export, reexport, or transfer of items subject to the EAR without a licence if the exporter knows or has reason to know that the item will be used for a 'military end-use' by a 'military end-user' in China, Russia, Burma (Myanmar), or Venezuela. 'Military end-use' includes incorporation into a military item, supporting military intelligence activities, or contributing to operation/maintenance/production of military items. The BIS Military End-User (MEU) List (Supplement No. 7) identifies specific parties requiring prior approval.
sections.notes: AI systems with dual-use or surveillance capabilities trigger § 744.21 analysis for Chinese, Russian, and Burmese end-users. Red flags include: buyer is military or affiliated with military; item has military application described in purchase documentation. Paraphrased — consult eCFR.

sections.title: Restrictions Related to Certain Emerging and Foundational Technologies
sections.summary: Section 744.22 was added to implement ECRA's mandate to control 'emerging and foundational technologies' essential to US national security. Items identified as emerging or foundational technologies may be subject to controls not yet reflected in specific ECCN entries. The interagency process under ECRA § 1758 is ongoing; exporters should monitor Federal Register notices for newly identified technologies. AI and machine learning are among the categories under review.
sections.notes: This is the regulatory hook for AI and advanced computing controls not yet codified in specific ECCNs. Monitoring BIS Federal Register notices is essential. Paraphrased — consult eCFR.

sections.title: Restrictions on Certain Military-Intelligence End-Uses and End-Users
sections.summary: Section 744.23 restricts exports to entities engaged in military-intelligence activities — i.e., activities for or on behalf of a military intelligence organisation of a government in China, Russia, Burma, or Venezuela. Licence requirements apply regardless of the ECCN of the item. AI/surveillance capabilities are of particular concern. BIS identifies specific entities on the MEU list.
sections.notes: Distinct from § 744.21 (military end-use) — this covers military intelligence activities specifically. AI systems with surveillance, facial recognition, or signals intelligence capabilities face heightened review. Paraphrased — consult eCFR.

sections.title: Embargoes and Other Special Controls
sections.summary: Part 746 implements country-specific export control restrictions, often in coordination with OFAC sanctions. Key provisions include: § 746.2 (Cuba), § 746.4 (Iran), § 746.5 (North Korea), § 746.6 (Syria), § 746.7 (Russia — broad restrictions on dual-use items including energy sector), § 746.8 (Belarus — mirrors Russia restrictions), § 746.9 (China advanced computing and semiconductor controls). Most licence exceptions are unavailable for countries under Part 746 restrictions. OFAC and BIS controls operate in parallel — both must be checked.
sections.notes: Russia restrictions (§ 746.7) introduced February 2022 and substantially expanded since. China advanced computing controls (§ 746.9) introduced October 2022 with subsequent amendments. Always check current text — Part 746 is frequently amended. Paraphrased — consult eCFR.

sections.title: Licence Exception ENC — Encryption Items
sections.summary: Licence Exception ENC authorises the export and reexport of certain encryption commodities, software, and technology without a licence to most destinations, subject to eligibility requirements. Key categories: (1) Items subject to EI (encryption items) or 'E' status — eligible after 30-day BIS review; (2) Mass-market encryption items (§ 740.17(b)(3)) eligible without review to most destinations; (3) Key management and intrinsically-safe encryption. ENC is NOT available for E:1/E:2 countries. Entity List parties require BIS authorisation. Annual self-classification reports may be required.
sections.notes: Most commercial software with standard encryption (AES-256, TLS) qualifies as mass-market under § 740.17(b)(3). However, items that use encryption AND have advanced computing capabilities (3A090, 4A090) may face additional controls that override ENC eligibility. Paraphrased — consult eCFR.
